coredata.dc:title: Productivity augmentation of rotating wick solar still using different designs of porous breathable belt and quantum dots nanofluid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University.
coredata.prism:publisher: THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdullah, A.S.
Alqsair, U.
Aljaghtham, Mutabe S.
Kabeel, A.E.
Omara, Z.M.
Essa, Fadl A.
coredata.dc:description: The problem of freshwater shortage is increasing a day after day. Solar distillers are suitable devices to overcome this problem for remote areas and small families. However, these devices suffer from their low productivities. Here, two different modified designs for the distillers of rotating wick types were tested. One distiller contained a rotating wick belt with a path of “L” character shape (l-RWSS), and the other with a path of “LC” shape (LC-RWSS). In addition, the wick type was investigated for black jute and cotton wicks. Moreover, the effect of belt-rotation direction (clockwise and counterclockwise) on the still performance has been studied. The period of wick belt rotation was tested (ON = 5 min and OFF = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min). Also, the impact of using graphene quantum dots nanofluid on the distiller performance was investigated. Experimental results obtained that the jute wick obtained more distillate than the cotton cloth. Furthermore, the productivity of LC-RWSS with counterclockwise movement of wick belt was always larger than that with clockwise direction under all different OFF times. Also, the LC-RWSS (jute wick-counterclockwise-0.1 rpm) increased the freshwater productivity by about 17 % over l-RWSS. Besides, the LC-RWSS provided higher accumulated daily productivity than l-RWSS by 19 % and 17 % with and without nanofluid, respectively. Also, the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.018 and 0.021 $/L for LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wick solar still
Nanofluids
Solar desalination
Rotating wick

Performance of LC-RWSS with various wick belt materials and counterclockwise motion
The thermal performance of the condenser (glass back side wall)
The effects of quantum dots nanofluids
Comparison among the results of this study and previous ones

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Both rotating wick solar stills (a) LC rotating wick solar still, (b) LC wick belt (back), (c) LC wick belt (front), (d) L rotating wick solar still, and (e) L wick belt.
Photo of experimental set up.
Scheme of the rotating wick solar stills.
The rotating drum with the rough surface.
Hourly solar radiation and temperatures of the tested solar stills at OFF time = 0 min.
The hourly and accumulative output yield of the tested solar stills at OFF time = 0 min.
Effect of different rotational directions of wick belt on LC-RWSS productivity.
Effect of different wick materials of wick belt on LC-RWSS productivity.
Hourly ambient temperature and temperatures of the glass walls and cover of LC rotating wick solar still at OFF time = 0 min.
Hourly productivity obtained from the glass walls and cover of the LC rotating wick solar still at OFF time = 0 min.
The hourly and accumulated output yield of the solar stills at OFF time = 30 min and ON time = 5 min.
The solar radiation and daily productivity of both solar stills under different OFF times and constant ON time of 5 min.
The increase in daily productivity of solar stills with and without quantum dots nanofluid under different OFF times and constant ON time of 5 min.
The thermal efficiency of solar stills under different conditions.
The solar radiation and daily productivity of both stills under different OFF times and constant ON time of 5 min.
The increase in daily productivity of solar stills with and without quantum dots nanofluids under different OFF times and constant ON time of 5 min.

row.entry:
Increase in productivity
(without quantum dots nanofluid), %
Increase in productivity (with quantum dots nanofluid), %

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Capital costs of stills’ components.
Details of calculating the economic analysis of desalination system.
Reasons of the high productivity obtained in our study compared to Haddad’s study.

row.entry: The wick belt is fixed vertically only.
The wick belt is fixed horizontally and vertically.
This leads to more evaporation and condensation which means more productivity.
Lower speed means more time for evaporation.
1.224 m length × 0.3 m width. (still width = 50 cm).
3.4 m length × 0.46 m width. (still width = 50 cm).
The more area of the belt, the higher amount of evaporation.
The driver roller is connected with the motor by belt. The belt has two openings in the back wall.
The driver roller is connected directly with the motor. So, there is only one opening in side wall of still.
The leakage occurrence is more controlled in our study.
Continuously rotating wick belt. He ensures the wet of the belt but does not test to which extend the wick belt is wet.
This leads to make an optimization for the time the wick belt is wet and dry. This enhances the productivity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of this study with previous investigations working on solar stills with wicks and nanomaterial.

row.entry: Double slope multi–wick solar still with black cotton wicks
Horizontal and vertical rotating wick belt inside a solar still fed by nanofluids

head.ce:title: Productivity augmentation of rotating wick solar still using different designs of porous breathable belt and quantum dots nanofluid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The problem of freshwater shortage is increasing a day after day. Solar distillers are suitable devices to overcome this problem for remote areas and small families. However, these devices suffer from their low productivities. Here, two different modified designs for the distillers of rotating wick types were tested. One distiller contained a rotating wick belt with a path of “L” character shape (l-RWSS), and the other with a path of “LC” shape (LC-RWSS). In addition, the wick type was investigated for black jute and cotton wicks. Moreover, the effect of belt-rotation direction (clockwise and counterclockwise) on the still performance has been studied. The period of wick belt rotation was tested (ON = 5 min and OFF = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min). Also, the impact of using graphene quantum dots nanofluid on the distiller performance was investigated. Experimental results obtained that the jute wick obtained more distillate than the cotton cloth. Furthermore, the productivity of LC-RWSS with counterclockwise movement of wick belt was always larger than that with clockwise direction under all different OFF times. Also, the LC-RWSS (jute wick-counterclockwise-0.1 rpm) increased the freshwater productivity by about 17 % over l-RWSS. Besides, the LC-RWSS provided higher accumulated daily productivity than l-RWSS by 19 % and 17 % with and without nanofluid, respectively. Also, the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.018 and 0.021 $/L for LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuity of life for all living things on the planet is closely related to the presence and abundance of water. Despite the importance of water, there are many creatures that suffer from a lack of water on Earth [1]. And because human civilization is at the peak of its progress now, scientists have cooperated to provide many solutions to overcome the problem of water shortage on the planet to ensure the continuity of life without disruption. Scientists, for example, have presented different types of methods that desalinate sea and ocean water into fresh water that can be used for drinking and washing for humans and animals, agriculture, and industry…etc. One of these methods used globally: reverse osmosis, stage flashing, effect boiling, humidification-dehumidification, electrodialysis, and solar stills [2,3]. The solar energy is the base of all renewable energy sources [4–8]. Solar stills are useful for simple family use and in places where solar energy is available, but they suffer from a low amount of productivity [9–11].

Some modifications that have contributed to improving the productivity and efficiency of the solar still are represented in the use of nanomaterials [12–16], quantum dots nanofluids [17], condensers [18–20], heat exchanger [21], reflectors [22], solar collectors [23], sun-tracking systems [24], solar ponds [25], volcanic rocks [26], wick materials [27], rotating parts [28–30], coated surfaces [31,32], phase change materials (PCM) [33–35], half barrel and corrugated surfaces [27], desiccant [36] and glass cooling [37]. Also, the quantum dots nanofluids were used for the solar systems due to their enhanced properties [38–41]. All these modifications can be summarized in a parameter of increasing the temperature difference between water and glass and decreasing the water depth of distiller [42–44]. The distiller yield would be increased with lowering the water depth and increasing the temperature difference [25,37,45,46]. In addition, various types of distillers were conducted to improve their performance. So, the solar distillers of plastic water purifier [47], disc [48], trays [49–52], stepped [53–55], drum [56,57], spherical [58], tubular [59–62], double-effect [63–65], vertical [66], pyramid [67,68], still with condensers [18,19], corrugated [69,70], and multi-stage [71] were conducted. The wick-type distiller is a distiller with burlap material spread over an evaporative surface to improve the vaporization of distiller. Some of burlap materials used were jute, pin-finned material, stones, rubber, cotton, metal pieces, and sponges were investigated [72].

As well studied and reported, utilizing the condenser has enhanced the distiller performance [71]. Reddy et al. [73] has found that the yield of a wick distiller was enhanced by 15–25 % via using the condenser. A thorough theoretical research was conducted by Pal et al. [74] to examine the effectiveness of a multi-wick double slope solar still. They achieved energy and exergy efficiency of 35 % and 3.83 %, respectively. The impact of several wicks, including jute and black cotton wick, was studied [75]. The results showed that the black cotton wick was more productive than the jute wick. Matrawy et al. [76] examined the performance of the solar still with corrugated wick basin liner. They concluded that the productivity of the solar distiller was improved when using wicks, reflectors, and nanofluids.

It was investigated how the productivity of solar stills operating in various water depths was affected by the use of two stacks of jute and black cotton wicks [77]. Compared to the pile of black cotton cloth, the pile of jute cloth produced more. Arunkumar et al. [78] examined the distiller output when coating the evaporative surface by CuO nanomaterials and using sponges. They demonstrated that using sponges was less effective than coating the absorber with CuO nanomaterials. To speed up the distillers’ evaporation process, Agrawal and Rana [79] added several floating wicks in the shape of a V. A 56.62 percent increase in distiller productivity was achieved. Gad et al. [80] introduced rotating belt horizontally inside a simple tilted wick distiller.

Therefore, two modified designs were made in an attempt to increase the wick distiller productivity without increasing the occupied horizontal area of the land but by optimizing the use of the vertical space only. The novelty of this research can be pointed by testing two various designs (L design and LC design) of rotating wick distillers. Both designs are based on the use of burlap material in the horizontal, vertical, and inclined space within the distiller space. In both designs, the burlap material was used as a rotary belt on the previously marked tracks (L and LC tracks). In addition, the wick type was investigated for black jute and cotton wicks. Moreover, the effect of belt-rotation direction (clockwise and counterclockwise) on the still performance has been studied. Furthermore, the belt was run for a fixed period (ON time = 5 min) and stopped for different periods (OFF times = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min) to study its effect on the performance of the distiller. Finally, the impact of using graphene quantum dots nanofluid of still yield was examined.

In be able to test the aforementioned variables (in the last paragraph in introduction section), an experimental integrated test bed was manufactured, installed, and assembled from solar stills modified with the new designs with the presence of a conventional solar still to be measured with, with the presence of some measuring devices and tools that help the continuity of the operation of the distillers such as a tank of salt water and valves…etc. These details are shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 . The tank of salt water was 0.5 m diameter × 1.0 m height. The modified distillers were nominated by l-RWSS (for the distiller with rotating belt in the form of L) and LC-RWSS (for the distiller with rotating belt in the form of LC). A schematic diagram for the details of distillers is obtained in Fig. 3 .

The basin area of l-RWSS was 0.5 m2. The main body of l-RWSS was made of 1.5 mm-thick galvanized steel. Also, the four distillers’ walls were built from glass (4 mm thickness) as shown from Fig. 1 and Fig. 2. The front and back walls were 15 and 95 cm respectively (Fig. 3). The distiller cover was built from glass (4 mm thickness). The distiller was involved inside a wooden box with a space of 5 cm between them. This space was filled with sawdust to work as an insultation. The still had 4 rotating pulleys to rotate the belt (Fig. 3). The condensed droplets were collected on 4 troughs fixed on the inner glass walls of the distiller.

LC-RWSS had the same land area and made of the same material of l-RWSS. Besides, the front and back walls of LC-RWSS were 40 and 95 cm respectively (Fig. 3). LC-RWSS had 6 rotating pulleys to rotate the belt (Fig. 3). LC-RWSS had a rotating wick belt with “L” track ended by a chamfer. This design was aimed to enhance the vaporization inside the basin.

The basin of the tested distillers had a height of 10 cm from all sides, and this part was exposed to solar radiation, so it transfers the thermal energy to the base and from it to the water. In addition, the front part of the modified distiller was directly exposed to solar radiation. Therefore, the base of the distillers was made of galvanized steel.

The belt was constructed from the tension of the burlap material as it has the width of the distiller and the length of the belt. The belt length was 3.4 and 3.64 m for l-RWSS and LC-RWSS, respectively. The belt wide was 0.46 m for both stills. The surface of the rotating rollers has been roughened to ensure that the burlap does not slide over them without rotation (Fig. 4 ). All components that require electricity to work have been connected to a 6-watt DC motor with solar panels to dispense with traditional energy.

In June and July of 2019, the experiments were carried out at Kafrelsheikh University, Egypt. To receive more sun energy, the bases of the solar stills were oriented east–west. The testbed was boosted by the measuring tools to report the values of solar intensity, temperatures, and distillate quantity. The time of experimental testing began at 08:00 am and still through the daytime to 06:00 pm. Also, the distillate was reported hourly through this time. The water depth inside distillers was 1 cm. A small, graded glass tube with a letter L shape was installed at the back side of the distiller to show the water height. A valve was used to control the amount of feed water from the feed tank. The feeding process was done every half hour to stabilize the water height at 1 cm. The bottom side of belt touches the basin water, and the belt rotates at 0.1 rpm. First, the effect of the various designs (l-RWSS and LC-RWSS) was tested. After that, the wick type was investigated for black jute and cotton wicks. Then, the effect of belt-rotation direction (clockwise and counterclockwise) on the still performance has been studied. Furthermore, the belt was run for a fixed period (ON time = 5 min) and stopped for different periods (OFF times = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min) to study its effect on the performance of the distiller. Finally, the impact of using graphene quantum dots nanofluid of still yield was examined.

The solar intensity was determined by a solarimeter (0 – 5000 W/m2). Also, an anemometer (0.4 – 30 m/s) was utilized to measure the air velocity. In addition, k-type thermocouples were used to know the temperatures at various points of test-rig. The signals coming from the thermocouples were translated into digital temperature values with the help of Arduino plate. Moreover, calibrated flasks (2 L capacity and 5 mL accuracy) were utilized to collect and quantize the distillate productivity of distillers.

The hourly irradiance and temperature for the distillers are shown in Fig. 5 at OFF time = 0 min. It is obtained from Fig. 5 that the LC-RWSS had higher water temperature than that of the l-RWSS by about 0 – 3.5 °C. This could be happened due to covering the L absorber by wick material with a percent larger than that of the LC absorber. This Therefore, some transmitted radiations were absorbed by the wick material in the l-RWSS with a percent more than that of the LC-RWSS. In addition, in the LC-RWSS, there was part of the basin water that was exposed directly to the incident radiations. This helped to raise the water temperature of LC-RWSS more than that of the l-RWSS. Moreover, it is found from Fig. 5 that both l-RWSS and LC-RWSS had similar glass and water temperatures at 08:00 am and 06:00 pm. Besides, the high vaporization of LC-RWSS led to raise its glass temperature compared to that of the l-RWSS by 0 – 1.5 °C.

Moreover, Fig. 6 presents the changes of hourly and total productivities for both still at OFF time = 0 min. The productivity of both distillers began to increase from zero in the morning to its maximum at noon time. The maximal values of yield were 1100 and 985 mL for LC-RWSS and l-RWSS distillers at 01:00 pm, respectively. After that, the distillers’ yield began to decrease with time due to the decline in the solar radiation as shown in Fig. 5. Also, Fig. 6 demonstrates that the hourly yield of l-RWSS was lower than that of the LC-RWSS. This improvement is referred to three main reasons. The first reason is the main reason which is considered as the surface area ready to absorb solar radiation for LC-RWSS is greater than that of l-RWSS. For example, the l-RWSS has a surface area around 0.81 m2 compared to 1.16 m2 for the LC-RWSS, Fig. 3. Besides, LC-RWSS had vaporization area than that of l-RWSS which raised the rate of evaporation of LC-RWSS. For instance, the evaporative surface area of the L still is 1.17 m2. As the wick is suspended between the pulleys, so evaporation occurs from both surfaces of the wick belt. Then, the total area of evaporation can be considered as 2 × 1.17 = 2.34 m2 for l-RWSS. While the evaporative surface area of LC-RWSS is 1.52 m2. The vaporization happened from both wick sides due to suspending the wick on the rollers. Then, the total vaporization area equals 2 × (1.52–0.125) + 0.125 = 2.9 m2. As a result, the evaporation rate inside the LC-RWSS is greater than that of the l-RWSS due to the high evaporation surface area. Additionally, the LC-RWSS had more condensation compared to that of l-RWSS. For instance, the l-RWSS had four condensation surfaces of the top glass cover, two glass sides, and back glass side. Also, the glass vertical front of LC-RWSS is a condensation surface. Additionally, the glass back side temperature was lower than that of top glass by 0 – 7.5 °C. The LC-RWSS had fewer temperatures for its left and right sides than that of the top glass by 0 – 2.5 °C. This is because the vertical sides were not exposed to the direct solar radiation.

Furthermore, the total productivity of the LC rotating wick solar still is more than that of the L rotating wick distiller as demonstrated in Fig. 6. The daily total yield was 6700 mL/m2 for the L rotating wick distiller compared to 7700 mL/m2 for the LC rotating wick distiller with an enhancement of 15 %. This is because the evaporating surface area and the heat transfer coefficients are increased via using the wick materials. What is more, the condensation area inside the LC still is higher than that of the L rotating wick still. In addition, according to the literature of [81], the average daily productivity of the conventional basin solar still is about 2500 mL/day. As a result, comparing our results with the results of [81], the productivity of the solar still can be improved by 168 % and 208 % when using the design of the present study of L and LC rotating wick solar stills, respectively. This improvement is referred to the following reasons. The first reason is the minimum amount of the saline water over the wick surface. Hence, the evaporation rate of the water inside the rotating wick still is high compared with the conventional basin still. The second reason is the rotating wick belt breaks the boundary layer of the basin water surface. This improved the vaporization of distiller. Also, rotating the wick belt leads to forced vaporization mode, accordingly the rate of vaporization is increased. Also, the high wick evaporative area and high exposed area to irradiation in l-RWSS and LC-RWSS compared to the conventional basin still.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of LC-RWSS with different rotational directions of wick belt
ce:section.ce:para: Here, in this subsection, the influence of changing the rotation direction of wick belt was tested to obtain the best operating conditions for LC-RWSS performance. As a result, the increase in distillate yield of LC-RWSS under constant ON time (5 min) and various OFF times with the directions of clockwise and counterclockwise of the jute cloth belt is demonstrated in Fig. 7 . It is observed from Fig. 7 that rotating the jute belt of LC-RWSS counterclockwise provided better yield than that with clockwise under all OFF times. This maybe due to that in the case of counterclockwise, the belt slides such that the highly saturated belt side was facing the back glass cover of minimum glass temperature. In addition, the wet side of wick belt was facing the back glass surface and not exposed directly to the solar energy. As a result, this surface loses some water due to gravity and vaporization. Then, this belt surface would be exposed directly to solar energy with relatively low conveyed water, so the evaporation process was fast. While, at clockwise motion, the belt surface which is exposed directly to solar energy was saturated with water more than that in the case of counterclockwise motion. Under 30 min OFF time of belt, the yield rise of LC-RWSS was 17 % and 15 % for counterclockwise and clockwise motions, respectively. Moreover, with increasing the OFF period of wick belt, the distillate yield of LC-RWSS with clockwise motion was close to that with counterclockwise motion. This was due to that the water was flowing to the basin because of gravity effect, which makes the effect of rotation direction small. Therefore, under 60 min OFF period, the increase in productivity of LC-RWSS with counterclockwise and clockwise motions was 14.2 % and 13.8 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of LC-RWSS with various wick belt materials and counterclockwise motion
ce:section.ce:para: Here, in this subsection, the impact of using various wick materials (jute and cotton wicks) on the LC-RWSS performance was investigated. So, the increase in distillate yield of LC-RWSS with jute and cotton cloths and counterclockwise motion is demonstrated in Fig. 8 . It is revealed from Fig. 8 that the daily yield increase of LC-RWSS with jute cloth was higher than that with cotton cloth for all OFF periods except at 60 min. This is maybe due to the high-water amount carried by the cotton cloth rather than the jute wick. This is due to that the jute material has enlarged offsets of lines rather than that of the cotton. Moreover, with increasing the OFF period of wick belt, the distillate yield of LC-RWSS with jute was close to that with cotton. This was due to that the water was flowing to the basin because of gravity effect, which makes the effect of wick materials small. Under OFF period of 0 min (continuous rotation), the yield of LC-RWSS was increased by 15 % and 11 % when using jute and cotton cloths, respectively. While the yield of LC-RWSS was raised by 17 % and 14 % when using jute and cotton cloths, respectively at 30 min OFF period. Whilst the yield of LC-RWSS was raised by 15.3 % and 14.2 % when using jute and cotton cloths, respectively at 60 min OFF period. This is due to the fact that at high stops, it causes dry points in the jute belt, which reduces its productivity somewhat, while the cotton belt remains completely wet because the cotton carries a large amount of water at the beginning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thermal performance of the condenser (glass back side wall)
ce:section.ce:para: It was observed from the above experiments that the LC-RWSS had fewer temperatures for the vertical glass walls (back, right, and left surfaces) than that of the above glass cover. Therefore, we focused on studying the thermal analysis of these surfaces. As a result, the changes of temperatures of surrounding air and vertical surfaces with glass cover for LC-RWSS at 0 min OFF time are demonstrated in Fig. 9 . The temperatures were in a like behavior of the irradiance. As the solar radiation increases, the temperatures increase and vice versa. Also, it was found that the glass cover temperature was higher than that of the vertical glass back surface by 0.5 – 7.5 °C. This happened due to the glass back surface did not face the sun directly. As a result, the vertical glass back surface worked as an internal condenser for the LC-RWSS. In addition, the glass cover temperature was higher than that of the right and left surfaces of glass vertical walls by around 0.5 – 2.5 °C. Additionally, through the morning time, the glass left surface had lower temperature than that of the glass right surface. This is due to facing the glass right surface directly to the sun. This action was inversed through the afternoon time. There was a time (around 01:00 pm) that the temperatures of both the right and left sides were equal due to the direct beams of solar radiations on both surfaces.

Moreover, Fig. 10 introduces the changes of yields provided by the vertical glass surfaces of LC-RWSS at 0 min OFF time. It should be mentioned that the total yield of LC-RWSS is the yields’ summation for the top glass cover, right glass surface, left glass surface, and back glass surface. According to the results of Fig. 10, it was reported that the back glass surface, top glass cover, and both side surfaces (right and left surfaces) participated by 38 %, 29 %, and 33 % of the total yield obtained by LC-RWSS, respectively. As a result, the back glass surface provided the highest percentage of productivity due to its low temperature compared to the other surfaces (Fig. 9). This due to the big difference in glass-water temperature of the back glass surface over that of the other surfaces. Also, through the morning period, the productivity of the glass right surface was lower than that of the glass left surface, and vice versa through the afternoon period. This action occurred due to falling the solar radiations directly on the right and left surfaces through the morning and afternoon periods, respectively.

Here, Fig. 11 shows the changes of hourly and total yields for distillers under 30 min OFF time and 5 min ON time. It was found that the maximal yields of l-RWSS and LC-RWSS were 1100 and 1300 mL, respectively at 01:00 pm. Moreover, the total yields of l-RWSS and LC-RWSS were 8200 and 9600 mL/m2, respectively. Therefore, the enhancement percentage reached 17 %.

More results for the effect of operating the wick belt under various OFF times are demonstrated in Fig. 12 and Table 1 . It is worth to mention that the tests of different conditions were conducted under similar weather conditions as illustrated in Fig. 12 and Table 1. The daily yield of distiller was increased with increasing the OFF time from 0 till 30 min, and it was declined after that till 60 min. Fig. 12 and Table 1 obtained that the LC-RWSS had bigger yield than the l-RWSS under all OFF times investigated. This was due to the improved vaporization and condensation of LC-RWSS compared to that of the l-RWSS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of quantum dots nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: Here, in this section, the impact of utilizing quantum dots nanofluids was investigated. The increase in productivity for distillers with and without nanofluids are shown in Fig. 13 and Table 2 . It was found that using the quantum dots nanofluids were doing efficiently with increasing the belt operating ON time. Under zero OFF time, the yield of LC-RWSS was enhanced by 18 % and 15 % with and without nanofluids, respectively, which means that using nanofluids improved the yield by only 3 % (Table 2). Whilst the yield improvement of LC-RWSS was reported as 19 % and 17 % with and without quantum dots nanofluids, respectively at OFF time = 30 min. This means that the impact of graphene quantum dots nanofluid was declined to only 2 % as demonstrated in Fig. 13 and Table 2. Under 30 min OFF period, without nanofluid, the daily accumulated yields of the LC-RWSS and l-RWSS were 9600 mL/m2 and 8200 mL/m2 respectively with an improvement percentage of 17 %. With nanofluids, the yields of the LC-RWSS and l-RWSS were 9900 mL/m2 and 8300 mL/m2 respectively with an improvement percentage of 19 %. In addition, under 60 min OFF time, the yield enhancement was reported as 15 % to 14.2 % with and without quantum dots nanofluid, respectively, which means that the impact of nanofluids was decreased to only 0.8 %.

The thermal efficacy of solar distillers is an important parameter that determines to what extend the distiller is useful and economic or not. The thermal efficiency is determined by [81]:
(1)
η d
( e f f i c i e n c y )
=
∑
m ̇
( p r o d u c t i o n
)
×
h
fg
( l a t e n t
h e a t )
∑ A
( a r e a
)
× I
s o l a r
r a d i a t i o n
+
P M
( e l e c t r i c
m o t o r
p o w e r
)

The details of thermal efficiency under different operating conditions are shown in Fig. 14 . It can be seen that the thermal efficiency took a similar behavior of the daily yield rise. In addition, it can be said that the thermal efficiency did not depend on the DC motor power because it had small value of 6 W. While it is observed that the efficiency relied mainly on the OFF times of the wick belt. It is concluded from Fig. 14 that the LC-RWSS with nanofluids had higher efficiency than that without nanofluids and l-RWSS. For example, the l-RWSS had a thermal efficiency of 74 % under zero OFF time of belt. Also, the thermal efficiency of LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids was 79.5 % and 77 %, respectively under zero OFF time of belt. Besides, under 30 min OFF time, l-RWSS had a thermal efficiency of 82 %. Also, the thermal efficiency of LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids was 88 % and 86 %, respectively under 30 min OFF time. Finally, at 60 min OFF time, the l-RWSS had a thermal efficiency of 72 %. Additionally, the thermal efficiency of LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids was 77.3 % and 76 %, respectively under 60 min OFF time. Therefore, it is concluded that the highest thermal efficiency of distillers was obtained at 30 min OFF time.

As well known, the performance of distillation systems depends on the cost of the system and distilled water. Table 3 presents the details of fixed costs for both distillers. In addition, the details of calculating the economic analysis are obtained in [44]. Regarding the equations presented in Table 4 , the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.027 $/L for l-RWSS. Also, the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.018 and 0.021 $/L for LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison among the results of this study and previous ones
ce:section.ce:para: A single comparison among the results of this study and Haddad’s study is presented. The efficiency of LC-RWSS is 88 % compared to 66 % for that of Haddad’s study [82]. This difference and enhancement of present distiller performance is considered due to the reasons in Table 5 .

More detailed comparisons among the results of this study and literature studies are introduced also to obtain the merits of the present study over the other studies. The complete comparison is introduced in Table 6 . The proposed modifications were confirmed as significant performance-improvement parameters. The proposed modifications were using rotating wick belt with two different designs of L and LC paths. Also, utilizing graphene quantum dots nanofluids has improved the performance of LC-RWSS.

Here, in this work, two modified designs of rotating wick solar distillers (L design and LC design) were tested. In addition, the wick type was investigated for black jute and cotton wicks. Moreover, the effect of belt-rotation direction (clockwise and counterclockwise) on the still performance has been studied. Furthermore, the belt was run for a fixed period (ON time = 5 min) and stopped for different periods (OFF times = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min) to study its effect on the performance of the distiller. Also, the impact of using graphene quantum dots nanofluid of still yield was examined. The experimental results delighted that the proposed modifications were confirmed as significant performance-improvement parameters. Also, the design of LC-RWSS obtained better performance than either the l-RWSS or conventional distiller. In addition, the best output yield of LC-RWSS was provided when operating the belt under 5 min ON and 30 min OFF times either with or without graphene quantum dots nanofluids. Besides, the LC-RWSS had a total yield more than that of the l-RWSS by 19 % and 17 % with and without nanofluids, respectively. Under 30 min OFF period of belt, the efficacy of l-RWSS and LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids was 82 %, 88 %, and 86 %, respectively. Additionally, the LC-RWSS design had a merit of built-in inside condenser of glass back-wall surface, which participated in the total productivity of LC-RWSS by 38 % compared to 29 % and 33 % for the top cover and both side surfaces (right and left walls), respectively. Also, the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.018 and 0.021 $/L for LC-RWSS with and without nanofluids, respectively.

We have faced some technical operating difficulties through the experiments such as the misalignment of the rotating belts and the continuous maintenance for the driving motor and driving belt. Also, some of operating difficulties can be summarized in not fixing the wick material over the driving rollers of belt due to smoothing surface of rollers.

ce:para.ce:bold: Further studies are proposed as following:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Different designs for rotating belt to increase the absorption and evaporation area.
Adding external and internal reflectors to improve the rotating wick efficiency.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, Ministry of Education in Saudi Aria for funding this research work through the project number (IF2/PSAU/2022/01/21083).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: F.A. Essa Fadl A. Essa was born in Kafrelsheikh, Egypt, on May 15th, 1988. He received a bachelor’s degree in Mechanical Engineering from Kafrelsheikh University on 2010. Then, he received his master's degree on June 2nd, 2014 from Tanta University. In 2013, he spent one month at People’s Friendship University of Russia, Moscow. Also, he got his Ph.D. from School of Mechanical and Electronic Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, China on June 20th, 2018. To date, he works as an assistant professor, Faculty of Engineering at Kafrelsheikh University in Egypt. The main research fields are water desalination and solar energy applications.

Dr. AbdelKader Saad Abdalla is an Associate Professor at Mechanical Engineering Department at Prince Sattam bin Abdulaziz University, Alkharj, KSA. He had earned his Ph.D. and M.Eng. degrees in Mechanical Power Engineering from Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt. His Ph.D. dissertation was in the area of heat transfer “Theoretical and Experimental investigations of heat Transfer in Channel Flow with surface ejection or injection at different angles”. He had developed an excellent academic and professional experience in solar energy. He has published more than 18 technical papers in top ISI ranked journals in Desalination , solar energy and Engineering area. He is also serving in different committees and conferences.

Dr. Abd Elnaby Kabeel is a Professor at Mechanical Engineering Department at Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt. In June 1997, he graduated from Mechanical Power Engineering, University of Technology, Bratislava, Slovak Republic. He has done his PhD on renewable energy entitled,” Computational Modelling and Experimental Analysis for Triangular and Rectangular Fin-Absorbers Solar Air Collectors”. Dr. kabeel research background is in Renewable Energy Systems, Water Desalination, Environmental service projects, Refrigeration and Air Conditioning and Desiccant Dehumidification Systems. Dr. kabeel has published more than 241 technical papers in top ISI ranked journals, 33 conferences. Dr. kabeel has Supervision of the Theses “Masters and PhD Thesis have been discussed - number of these 30 Thiess” and “Masters and PhD Thesis under preparation for discussion- number of these 20 Thiess”.

Dr. Zakaria Mohamed Omara is a Professor at Mechanical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Kafrelsheikh University, Egypt. In February 2008, he graduated from Mechanical Power Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt. Dr. Omara’s research background is in Renewable Energy Systems, Water Desalination and Solar Energy Systems. Dr. Omara’s Reviewer in ten International Journals “ Desalination, Desalination and Water Treatment, Energy Conversion & Management, Solar Energy”. Dr. Omara has published more than 40 technical papers in top ISI ranked journals in Desalination , solar energy and Engineering area.
